title: Paris wildfire | US ‘heat dome’ | Trump ‘wants new coal’ - Carbon Brief
gist: A Fontainebleau wildfire near Paris and France's third heatwave of the summer coincide with 6.3GW of French nuclear capacity offline (14% of national demand) and record EU solar generation (52TWh in June); China's electricity load hit a first-of-year record of 1,500GW driven by data-centre and EV charging demand; US business groups press incoming UK PM Burnham to cut energy costs; and a Reuters columnist finds the US power system's H1 2026 output at an all-time high with clean power gaining share.

themes.theme: France's heat-driven nuclear and grid stress

insights.claim: 6.3 gigawatts of French nuclear power capacity was offline due to the heatwave, creating a supply gap equivalent to around 14% of national demand, while the Rhine River fell to its lowest levels in decades, limiting fuel barge flows to Germany and Switzerland.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Documents a recurring structural vulnerability where extreme heat simultaneously curtails nuclear cooling capacity and constrains river-based fuel logistics -- a compounding European energy-security risk during summer heatwaves relevant to power-price volatility forecasting.

insights.claim: Solar power generated a quarter of EU electricity in June 2026, a record 52 terawatt hours, according to thinktank Ember data cited by BusinessGreen.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A concrete record EU solar generation figure (52TWh, 25% of power) useful for benchmarking the pace of EU solar penetration and its role in offsetting nuclear/hydro shortfalls during heatwaves.

insights.claim: A wildfire near Fontainebleau, roughly 60km southeast of Paris, had scorched more than 800 hectares by Monday morning amid the region's third red-alert heatwave of the summer, with French President Macron saying 'all means' were being deployed to fight the 'exceptional scale' blaze.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Confirms wildfire risk has moved into densely populated regions near major European capitals, not just traditionally fire-prone southern Europe -- relevant for European property/casualty insurance risk repricing.
insights.locator: Wildfire rages near Paris as heatwave scorches Europe

themes.theme: China's power demand surge from data centres and EVs

insights.claim: China's electricity load hit a first-of-year record of 1,500 gigawatts last week, rising by 150GW since the start of July -- an increase roughly equivalent to Japan's entire power load -- driven partly by data-centre and EV charging demand.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A striking scale comparison (China added a Japan's-worth of load in two weeks) that quantifies the magnitude of China's simultaneous AI/data-centre and EV-driven electricity demand growth, relevant for forecasting Chinese power generation capex needs.
insights.locator: China power load hits early record as data, EV demand rises

insights.claim: Electricity demand from internet data services rose 44.6% in China over the first five months of 2026, while EV charging and battery-swapping services demand grew 56.8%, and every 1C increase in China's average summer temperature raises electricity demand by 40-50GW.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Quantifies three distinct demand drivers (data centres +44.6%, EV charging +56.8%, and a specific heat-sensitivity coefficient of 40-50GW per 1C) -- precise inputs for modeling China's near-term power generation and grid investment requirements.
insights.locator: China power load hits early record as data, EV demand rises

themes.theme: UK energy cost pressure on incoming PM Burnham

insights.claim: A CBI/Energy UK report found UK energy costs could be cut by a fifth and the economy boosted by £130bn by 2050 if legacy renewable subsidy levies were shifted off consumer bills onto general taxation, with 40% of surveyed firms saying they are cutting UK investment due to high energy costs (which run 45% above the G7 median).
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A concrete industry-backed policy proposal (moving subsidy costs to general taxation) with quantified upside (20% bill cut, £130bn GDP boost) that is a live near-term policy lever for the incoming Burnham government -- a key catalyst for UK industrial energy-cost competitiveness.
insights.locator: UK: Energy costs are your priority, business chiefs tell Andy Burnham

insights.claim: North Sea oil lobbyists wrote to more than 400 Labour MPs urging incoming PM Andy Burnham to allow more oil and gas drilling in UK waters, appealing to his reindustrialisation agenda just before he takes office.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Signals coordinated, large-scale industry lobbying pressure on the incoming government at a pivotal decision-making moment -- a leading indicator that North Sea licensing policy could shift favorably for oil and gas operators.
insights.locator: UK: Energy costs are your priority, business chiefs tell Andy Burnham

insights.claim: Energy secretary Ed Miliband reportedly wants to approve North Sea drilling to calm market jitters about his possible appointment as chancellor and to demonstrate he is not a net-zero 'zealot.'
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: If accurate, suggests even the most net-zero-identified senior UK minister may support new North Sea licensing as a political concession -- a signal that North Sea drilling approval is increasingly likely regardless of which Labour figure becomes chancellor.
insights.locator: UK: Energy costs are your priority, business chiefs tell Andy Burnham

themes.theme: US power system trends and heat dome

insights.claim: US total electricity output for H1 2026 reached 2,234 terawatt hours, the highest ever for a half-year period, up nearly 3% year-on-year, with clean power approaching 'primary status' due to solar strength and continued coal shrinkage even as natural gas 'remains indispensable.'
insights.speaker: Gavin Maguire
insights.so_what: Confirms continued structural growth in US clean power share despite federal policy headwinds under Trump, a data point supporting continued investment in US solar generation capacity even amid political rollback risk.
insights.quote: Natural gas remains indispensable, but the direction of travel is unmistakable: with each passing year, the US grid looks ⁠a little less ​fossil-fueled and a little more clean-powered.
insights.locator: Mid-year check-up on the US power system's vital signs

insights.claim: The US National Weather Service predicted more than 90 temperature records would be broken across the US within a week, most of them overnight heat records, as a heat dome moved from the western US eastward, hindering wildfire-fighting efforts in Colorado and Utah.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A concrete near-term US heat event forecast relevant for power-demand spike planning and wildfire-related insurance loss estimation in the affected states.
insights.locator: Heat dome expected to bring record temperatures across the US

insights.claim: New York's Democratic governor Kathy Hochul defended her decision to walk back the state's emissions targets.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Signals climate-policy retrenchment is spreading beyond Republican-controlled jurisdictions to Democratic-led states, a sign of broadening political resistance to aggressive near-term decarbonisation targets in the US.

insights.claim: Great Britain's grid operator NESO has brought in independent legal investigators after a whistleblower claimed control-room staff were warned against leaving a paper trail relating to efforts to stabilise the power system during record heatwave temperatures in late June, despite NESO's own statement that the system 'operated securely.'
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: An active whistleblower investigation into potential grid near-blackout cover-up is a material governance/regulatory risk for UK grid operator credibility, with possible policy implications for grid investment oversight.
insights.locator: Lawyers to investigate claims of GB power grid cover-up over blackout risk
